methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series B → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.9x) to $132M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Quantum Computing (found 0 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 5 same-sector peers exist in Quantum Computing, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

data.narrative:
Q-CTRL is a Series B quantum computing company that has raised $132M to date. The valuation analysis draws on a peer universe of 11 comparable companies across quantum computing—3 public (IonQ, Rigetti, D-Wave) and 8 private firms including Quantinuum and Infleqtion.

Given the nascent state of quantum computing and heterogeneity in business models and technology approaches, only one valuation method proved applicable. The stage-progression approach, which uses total capital raised as a baseline proxy and applies a typical Series B step-up multiplier of 1.9x, yields an implied valuation of $251M. This method assumes that capital deployed and equity dilution patterns at this stage follow historical venture patterns within the sector, though application carries inherent uncertainty in an emerging technology landscape. The peer-median method could not be applied; while 5 quantum computing peers exist in the comp set, none satisfied minimum thresholds for same-stage or adjacent-stage filtering (requiring 3+ datapoints), and a cross-sector median would introduce unacceptable noise given differences in technology maturity and commercialization timelines.

The analysis supports a valuation range of $213M (low) to $288M (high), centered on $251M. This single-method result reflects significant model limitation. Key uncertainties include the validity of stage-progression multipliers in an immature sector, the degree to which Q-CTRL's technology and market positioning align with public comparables, and the absence of narrowly comparable private transaction data. Additional operational metrics—revenue run-rate, customer concentration, and burn dynamics—would sharpen conviction in any point estimate.
